coredata.dc:title: Anticorrosive properties of Eucalyptus (Nilgiris) leaves extract on 2S grade aluminium in acid solutions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Desai, P.S.
Desai, Falguni
Patel, Adarsh
Parmar, Bhumika
coredata.dc:description: The study is on the application of Eucalyptus leaves extract (ELE) as anticorrosive properties for aluminium alloy in 0.5 M HCl by using potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), gravimetric weight loss, and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) methods. The corrosion inhibition characteristics of ELE on aluminium in HCl solution have been examined. This investigation showed the finest inhibition efficiencies of 95.0%, 92.0% and 89.3% that were obtained from weight loss, PDP and EIS at concentration of 0.5% ELE at 303 K respectively. As per Langmuir adsorption isotherm models, an extract of Eucalyptus leaves was adsorbed on the metal surface. The effect of the temperature on the corrosion behaviour with adding of the best concentration of ELE was studied in the temperature range 313–333 K. It was found that inhibition efficiency is independent of temperature. The negative Gibb's free energy and activation parameters of the ELE were recorded and the results indicated that the adsorption rates were spontaneous. The results obtained from PDP and EIS shows that the corrosion inhibition procedure is charge transfer controlled, and the ELE works as a mixed-type inhibitor. The findings of SEM (Scanning electron microscopy) and AFM (Atomic force microscopy) studies support the adsorption inhibition mechanism. The results of all the experiments conduct are consistent. The functional groups of the ELE were identified using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AFM
Corrosion
Eucalyptus
EIS
Inhibitors
Nilgiri
Polarization
SEM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Al corrosion loss in 0.5 M HCl with or without ELE at 303 K for 24 h.
Impact of inhibition efficiency of ELE for Al in 0.5 M HCl at 303 K for 24 h.
Arrhenius plots for Al corrosion in 0.5 M HCl with and without inhibitor.
Log ϴ versus Log C plot for ELE in 0.5 M HCl for 3 h.
Cinh/ϴ vs Cinh plot for ELE in 0.5 M HCl.
Time potential diagrams for aluminium in 0.5 M HCl in the absence and presence of various concentration of the ELE at 303 K.
Tafel graphs for Al in 0.5 M HCl with or without ELE.
Circuit fitted Bode graphs for Al in 0.5 M HCl with and without ELE (a) log Z Vs Log F (Hz) and (b) Phase angle Vs Log F (Hz).
Nyquist Al curves in 0.5 M HCl with and without Eucalyptus leaves extract.
Fitted circuit of EIS spectrum.
SEM micrographs of (a) Without Eucalyptus leaves extract and (b) With Eucalyptus leaves extracted at an optimum concentration (0.5%) on aluminium coupons in 0.5 M HCl.
AFM spectra of aluminium coupons (a) without inhibitor three dimensional (b) with inhibitor three dimensional images in 0.5 M HCl for 3 h at 303 K.
FT-IR spectrum of Eucalyptus leaves extract.
Metal composition of aluminium alloy.
Corrosion loss and surface coverage area of aluminium in 0.5 M HCl in Eucalyptus leaves extract for 3 h.
“Free energy adsorption (ΔG0 ads), heat of adsorption (Qads), entropy of adsorption (ΔS0 ads) and enthalpy of adsorption (ΔH0 ads)” containing inhibitors in 0.5 M HCl for 3 h.
Freundlich adsorption isotherm parameters for Al in 0.5 M HCl with ELE.
Potentiodynamic polarization values for Al in). 5 M HCl with and without the finest concentration of inhibitors at 303 K.
Impedance results for Al with and without ELE in 0.5 M HCl.
AFM information extracted from height profile for Al in the uninhibited and inhibited environment.

head.ce:title: Anticorrosive properties of Eucalyptus (Nilgiris) leaves extract on 2S grade aluminium in acid solutions

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cResearch Scholar, Department of Chemistry, Arts, Science and Commerce College, Kholwad, Kamrej Char Rasta 394185. Dist. Surat, Gujarat, INDIA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study is on the application of Eucalyptus leaves extract (ELE) as anticorrosive properties for aluminium alloy in 0.5 M HCl by using potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), gravimetric weight loss, and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) methods. The corrosion inhibition characteristics of ELE on aluminium in HCl solution have been examined. This investigation showed the finest inhibition efficiencies of 95.0%, 92.0% and 89.3% that were obtained from weight loss, PDP and EIS at concentration of 0.5% ELE at 303 K respectively. As per Langmuir adsorption isotherm models, an extract of Eucalyptus leaves was adsorbed on the metal surface. The effect of the temperature on the corrosion behaviour with adding of the best concentration of ELE was studied in the temperature range 313–333 K. It was found that inhibition efficiency is independent of temperature. The negative Gibb's free energy and activation parameters of the ELE were recorded and the results indicated that the adsorption rates were spontaneous. The results obtained from PDP and EIS shows that the corrosion inhibition procedure is charge transfer controlled, and the ELE works as a mixed-type inhibitor. The findings of SEM (Scanning electron microscopy) and AFM (Atomic force

microscopy) studies support the adsorption inhibition mechanism. The results of all the experiments conduct are consistent. The functional groups of the ELE were identified using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The food and packaging sectors make extensive use of aluminium. Aluminium is lightweight and a superconductor of electricity. It is also given precedence for the fabrication of automotive and marine components, fast ocean-going boat hulls, military hardware for submarines, liquified natural gas cargo tanks, etc., because of its better corrosion-resistant qualities [1]. Aluminium has been able to replace copper in many situations because of these features combined with other inherent properties. Due to the increased daily usage of alloys and metals in modern life, the issue of metal corrosion is of utmost concern. In aqueous solutions, aluminium has been seen to demonstrate passive behaviour. The processes that the passivating surface oxide layer, including ion movement in the oxide film, electron transfer from the metal to acceptor species, and metal ion transfer to the oxide/metal interface in solution, are thought to be responsible for metal corrosion [2].

HCl solutions are often utilized for aluminium's electrochemical and chemical etching, which frequently results in significant metal dissolution. H2SO4 and HCl are two cleaning agents often used in industrial applications to remove unwanted rust and scale. Several kinds of inhibitors are often utilized to limit acid consumption and metal dissolution. Most commonly utilized acid inhibitors are organic molecules made of sulfur, oxygen, and nitrogen, yet most of these compounds are poisonous and hazardous to the environment [3–5]. Inhibitory properties of the synthesized heterocyclic compounds have been discovered, and more research is being done on the heterocycles [6]. Although many synthetic compounds show strong anti-corrosive properties, there is a worldwide concern about their toxicity to both people and the environment, whether this toxicity occurs during the synthesis of the chemical or during its usage [7,8]. Because they are biodegradable, non-toxic, renewable resources of materials, affordable, and ecologically acceptable, the study is focused on using plant products as favourable eco-friendly inhibitors [9]. Most plant extracts are effective inhibitors of aluminium corrosion in acid and are used to treat corrosion problems without affecting the environment [10–17]. In the past, medicinal herbs were used to prevent corrosion in acid solution on aluminium alloy [18,19]. Hence, the development of novel and green corrosion inhibitors extracted from the plant has become one of the main directions of corrosion scientists [20].

The present research aims to check the inhibitive efficiency of ELE in 0.5 M hydrochloric acid solution to estimate its inhibition effectiveness. Tafel polarization, Mass loss, AFM, SEM, and EIS were applied to assess the inhibition potency of ELE. Before going further in the experiment, the following lines very briefly encompass the origin and significance of eucalyptus. The native origin of various species of the Eucalyptus is Australia, in countries like Tasmania, New Guinea, Timor and Moluccas area also these trees are found. In above mentioned places Eucalyptus cover huge part of forest. Knowing and recognizing the value of Eucalyptus, travelers carried seeds to the countries having similar tropical climate. History says that India got seeds of Eucalyptus from Australia. After planting the seeds in Nandi Hills, the next plantation of Eucalyptus was in the Nilgiri hills in Tamil Nadu in 1843 it came to be known as Nilgiri [21]. Because of its therapeutic benefits, Eucalyptus (Nilgiri) is commonly recognized all over the globe. The oil extract from the diverse part of the Nilgiri demonstrates antifungal, antibacterial, antioxidant, antiseptic, anticancer, and anti-inflammatory activity. Nilgiri is used to treat respiratory conditions, influenza, the common cold, congestion, and sinus because of these qualities. Other applications include aromatherapy, phytotherapy, cosmetics, food, and beverages [22].

Rectangular specimens of 2S grade aluminium alloy measuring 5.0 × 2.0 × 0.18 cm in size with an area of 0.2414 sq dm and a small hole of around 5 mm in diameter closed to the top edge were utilized to calculate the corrosion loss. The grease may be removed by using analytical-grade acetone to clean the aluminium surface, drying it in heated air using an air drier, and storing it in a desiccator until use. The metal composition of aluminium alloy is reflected in Table 1 .

The leaves harvested from Dang Forest in Gujarat state of India were washed in running water and sun-dried for about 15 days. In most of the reported cases, dried materials are preferred due to their longer preservation time compared to the fresh samples [23]. The leaves were then ground into powder after drying. Using a Soxhlet system, a 25 gm dry powder sample was extracted for 5 h with 350 mL of distilled ethanol. After the accomplishment of extraction, alcohol was removed by using a fractional distillation process, and the resulting solution was utilized as an inhibitor. The extraction of ELE was stored in transparent glass bottle and used up to three months. An inhibitor concentration between 0.1 and 0.5 percent was made.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characterization of eucalyptus leaves extract
ce:section.ce:para: A well-known FT-IR Bruker spectrophotometer was utilized to capture and monitor the FT-IR spectra for the extract of eucalyptus leaves, with wavelengths ranging between 4000 and 400cm−1.

The test samples were submerged in a 0.5 M HCl solution (hydrochloric acid) with and without inhibitors. Eucalyptus leaves extract was applied as an inhibitor in the concentration value between 0.1 and 0.5%. In each beaker holding 230 ml of the test solution, one sample alone was suspended by a glass hook and left exposed to the air at room temperature for 24 h before being cleaned with a chromic-phosphate solution [24]. The mean weight loss data was given in mg/sq. dm, and each experiment was done in triplicate.

The following formulae (1) and (2) were applied to compute the corrosion loss, surface coverage degree (θ), and inhibition efficiencies: The corrosion loss in the uninhibited system is represented by Wu, while Wi is represented as corrosion loss is inhibited.
(1)
η % =
Wu − Wi
W u
x
100
(2)
θ =
(
W u − W i
W u
)

A sample is subjected to polarization procedures to alter its current or potential, which are then recorded. There are two ways to do this: AC (alternating current) or DC (direct current). For the electrochemical investigation, 1cm2 Al metal specimens were exposed to a corrosive solution. Electrochemical measurements were made using a Galvanostat/Potentiostat from CH-instruments (model CHI608C) set to 303 K. The measurement was made using three-electrode cell systems. An additional Pt electrode was placed in 230 ml of a 0.5 M HCl solution, and Al metal was utilized as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The external current was provided automatically for the investigation and measured changes in potential. Tafel curves, a current potential diagram, show anodic and cathodic processes in an electrochemical cell. The slopes of the polarization curve were used to calculate the cathodic and anodic Tafel constant values. At corrosion potentials, impedance measurements were performed in the frequency range between 100 kHz and 10 MHz using a 5 mV signal amplitude perturbation. Potentiodynamic polarisation tests were performed with a 2 mV S −1 scan rate in the potential range of 1000 to 2000 mV. Before the EIS experiment, working electrode was dipped in 0.5 M HCl solution containing 0.1% to 0.5% ELE. This process was done for 68 min in the open circuit potential (OCP) to achieve a stationary condition, and temperature was maintained 303 K. Triplicates experiment were performed and average values were generated for further calculation.

For SEM (Scanning electron microscopy) examination, Al coupons were immersed with and without the finest ELE concentration, which was inundated at room temperature (303 K) for 24 h and was examined through the Hitachi company S3400N model. Al coupons were dipped in 0.5 M hydrochloric acid for 24 h at 303 K with and without ELE. Eucalyptus leaves are in the inhibited system at an optimal concentration (0.5%). SEM was conducted using the Hitachi S3400N model microscope mode at a 15,000 V accelerating voltage and a 250x magnification. With the help of a solver scanning probe microscope controller, AFM was carried out utilizing the Russian NT-MDT multimode.
Figs. 1-13 and Tables 2-7 illustrate the findings. To determine their protective ELE values were added at 0.5 M HCl.

Temperature effect:
Table 2 displays the findings of ELE inhibition efficiency and corrosion loss of Al in 0.5 M HCl acid at 313, 323, and 333 K. With the rise in ambient temperature from 313 K to 333 K led to substantial increases in corrosion loss. However, for each addition of Eucalyptus leaves extract, the corrosion loss decreased for every θ recorded. Low corrosion loss indicates strong inhibition effectiveness, and the inset shows that increasing ELE run in all θ cases will enhance inhibition effectiveness. With an increase in surface area (θ), the inhibitory efficacy at a constant dose of Eucalyptus leaves extracts is reduced. With 0.5 percent ELE added, an efficiency of 92.44 percent was achieved at 313 K, but this efficiency dropped to 63.6 and 33.9 percent for 323 and 333 K, correspondingly, due to the high rate of dissolution of the aluminium alloy's component by the corrosive medium.

Aluminium in 0.5 M HCl has a mean energy of activation (Ea) value of 22.96 kJ mol−1, according to Eq. (3), whereas inhibitor-containing acids have mean Ea values that are larger than those in uninhibited media (Table 3). Higher mean Ea values reveal the physical adsorption of inhibitors onto metal surfaces, raising the energy barrier for corrosion mechanism [27]. These values determined using Eq. (3), and the slope of the Arrhenius graph (Fig. 3 ) are almost identical.
(3)
L o g
P 2
P 1
=
E a
2.303 R
[
1
T 1
−
1
T 2
]

The following eq computed the adsorption of enthalpy and adsorption of entropy. (4) and (5), respectively. The positive ΔH0 ads values show the endothermic behaviour of Al dissolution in 0.5 M HCl solution. The fact that ΔH0 ads value rises as ELE concentration does shows that ELE concentration increases have an endothermic effect. ΔS0 ads is a measure of system disorder ness. Increasing the ΔS0 ads values with inhibitors exhibited that the activated complex during the rate-determining stage indicates dissociation instead of association and that a reduction in disorder occurs when reactants are transferred to the active complex [28–31].
(4)
Δ
H
a d s
0
=
E a
− R T
(5)
T
Δ
S
a d s
0
=
Δ
H
a d s
0
−
Δ
G
a d s
0

The following equation was used to compute the heat of adsorption values (Qads) (6). Table 3 clarifies that Qads values are always negative and vary between −11.17 to −18.74 kJ.mol−1. These negative outcomes demonstrate the adsorption rate and, thus, η% decreases with increasing temperature [32,33].
(6)
Q
a d s
= 2.303 R
[
L o g
(
θ 2
1 −
θ 2
)
− L o g
(
θ 1
1 −
θ 1
)
]
×
[
T 1
T 2
T 2
−
T 1
]

Many adsorption isotherms were used, but Freundlich and Langmuir's isotherm is the best extract from eucalyptus leaves. The equations (7) of Freundlich and (8) of the Langmuir adsorption isotherm below can represent the link between the inhibitor concentration and extent of surface coverage.
(7)
L o g
θ = n
L o g
C + L o g
K
(8)
C
i n h
θ
=
1
K
a d s
+
C
i n h

The slope values and linear correlation coefficient are reflected in Table 3. The fact that the values of R2 for both of these isotherms are near unity shows that the inhibitor molecule adheres to the Al surface as per the Langmuir model. Table 4 displays the K values determined from the graphs' interception. The following Eq. (9) [34,35] is used to compute K, which is linked to the ΔG0 ads adsorption:
(9)
Δ
G
0
=
− 2.303
RT
log 1000
K
a d s
Where Kelvin's absolute temperature is given by T, the universal gas constant by R, and R = 0.008314 kJ.mol−1. The isotherms have negative ΔG0 ads and agree on stable spontaneous adsorption of ELE [36,37]. Table 4 clarifies that as the temperature rises, ΔG0 ads values are in declining order (less negative), suggesting that the more effective inhibitors show a larger negative value. Securing a decline in inhibitory efficacy with increasing temperature suggests physical adsorption. It indicates that they should be firmly adsorbed on the metal surface. In general, ΔG0 ads values are less negative in comparison to −20.0 kJ.mol−1 imply physical adsorption, while values higher than −40.0 kJ.mol−1 reveal chemical adsorption [38,39]. The physical adsorption process is supported by ΔG0 ads values reported in this experiment being less than −20.0 kJ.mol−1. The findings demonstrated that the inhibition process includes the inhibition molecules adsorbing to the aluminium surface and inhibiting it. Cations will be more likely to adsorb when the surface charge is negative, but anion adsorption is more likely when the surface charge is positive. An essential factor to consider is how firmly adsorbed the Cl- ions in HCl may be on the surface. This enables the physical adsorption of inhibitor cations [40].

The deviation of open circuit potential (OCP) of Al surface in 0.5 M HCl were performed over time in the absence and presence of ELE within the solution until steady-state was achieved (Fig. 6 ). The figure shows that a general tendency is seen in OCP. The OCP values initially increased in the positive direction, and were then followed by small change in the quasi-static potential. This trend indicates that the corrosion reaction starts rapidly as the sample is dipped in the 0.5 M HCl and is confirmed with time, and then reaches a half-steady state within the time interlude examined. The lesser negative values suggest an increased corrosion rate. Positive OCP values increased with increase in the concentration of ELE in acid solution. This is an indication of the adsorption of ELE on the Al surface, which slightly influences the anodic side. The literature indicates that the inhibitors are classified as anodic or cathodic if the OCP in the presence of the inhibitor varies by at least ±85 mV, relative to the OCP in the absence of inhibitor [41]. The maximum potential shift towards the anodic site at the highest inhibitor concentration in its blank solution is 26 mV in 0.5 M HCl solution. This value is less than the 85 mV due to which the ELE act as mixed type.

To further understand the cathodic and anodic reactions of aluminium in the acid solution, Tafel plot tests were conducted with and without ELE of varying concentrations. The Tafel plots of aluminium alloy without and with inhibitors are reflected in Fig. 7 . Table 5 represents the parameters like Ecorr (corrosion potential), icorr (corrosion current density), and βa (anodic) and βc (cathodic) slopes which are taken from the Tafel plots. The inhibition effectiveness of ELE at each concentration was determined using the following equation.
(10)
η
( % )
=
[
i
c o r r
( u )
−
i
c o r r
( i )
i
c o r r
( u )
]
x
100

Since corrosion current values are negatively correlated with ELE concentrations, raising these concentrations typically slows down both cathodic and anodic aluminium alloy processes in acidic conditions. Lower icorr values clearly show higher inhibition efficacy of the ELE for Al alloy in HCl solutions in the presence of inhibitors. The Ecorr values on the current test do not shift in either direction, and the ELE spices have an equal impact on the βa and βc values. On the present examination, the Ecorr values don't shift towards any direction, and βa and βc values are equally impacted by the ELE spices. These are clear indicators that the tested inhibitors act as mixed-type inhibitors on the Al surface in 0.5 M HCl acid solution. Fig. 8 a-b depicts the Bode curves, where the linear slope sections rise as the amount of ELE rises, indicating the existence of ELE adsorbate on aluminium before the slide oxidizes [42]. Improved surface coverage (ϴ) is indicated by an increase in phase angles [43].

Additionally, the EIS approach was employed to analyse the corrosion behaviour of aluminium alloy specimens at various ELE concentrations, and the results are illustrated in Fig. 9 . In Fig. 9, it is obvious that the semicircles on the impedance curve explain it, showing that charge transfer is the primary mechanism by which corrosion occurs. Table 6 provides impedance parameters for aluminium in 0.5 M hydrochloric acid solution with and without Eucalyptus leaves extract. These parameters include Rct (charge transfer resistance), which is comparable to Rp (resistance potential), and the Cdl (double layer capacitance). Based on the Nyquist plot curve, an AC voltage with minor perturbations of 5 mV to 10 mV is applied in the system throughout a frequency range generally beginning from 100 kHz to 10 MHz, an equivalent circuit comprises information on Rs (solution resistance), Cdl, and Rct to represent the electrochemical cell including the metal sample, electrolyte system, as well as adsorbed inhibitors. The impedance data of Al in 0.5 M HCl are examined in terms of corresponding circuit model revealed in Fig. 10 , which comprises the double layer capacitance Cdl which is sited in parallel to the charge transfer resistance Rct, due to the charge transfer reaction [44]. The values of Rct have been calculated by the following relation [45]:
(11)
R
c t
= Z
(
a t
l o w
f r e q u e n c y
)
− Z
(
a t
h i g h
f r e q u e n c y
)
The Eq (13) expressed Cdl values [46]:
(12)
ω
R
c t
C
d l
= 1
where, ω = 2π
f
m a x
and the imaginary impedance component is maximum (Zmax). Eq. (14) has been used to determine the inhibitory efficacy of ELE from the Rct values [47].
(13)
η
( % )
=
[
R
c t
(
i n h
)
−
R
c t
R
c t
(
i n h
)
]
x 100
where Rct and Rct(inh) values are represented with and without ELE, respectively. Based on the impedance data in Table 6, there is no variation in the corrosion process, but there is a considerable change in the impedance modulus of Al in 0.5 M HCl media by expanding the width of the semicircle. With increasing ELE concentration, Cdl values decline. If water molecules at the electrode contacts are substituted with an extract of Eucalyptus leaves with a lower dielectric constant by adsorption, the capacitance may be reduced [48]. In contrast to the Cdl values, which decrease with increasing ELE concentration compared to an uninhibited system, the Rct values indicate the protective layer created on the aluminium surface by adsorption and an enhancement in the corrosion η% in acid solution. As a result, the electric double layer thickness generated on the aluminium surface increased, or the local dielectric constant was reduced when H2O molecules were replaced with inhibitor molecules [49,50].

SEM: Fig. 10a-b shows SEM images of an aluminium surface dipped in 0.5 M hydrochloric acid for 3 h with and without 5% Eucalyptus leaves extract. Images of the aluminium surface in Fig. 11 a without ELE and an inspection of the surface's roughness show obvious cavities and pits resulting from severe damage from 0.5 M HCl attack. Fig. 11b shows the microscopic picture taken when ELE was present; these micrographs demonstrated less destruction to the aluminium metal surface due to ELE molecules attaching to the surface.

AFM : An effective way for acquiring roughness statistics from different surfaces is AFM. AFM is becoming a widely used technique to investigate roughness [51]. Fig. 12 displays 3-D AFM images of the aluminium surface with and without inhibitors. Fig. 12a displays the aluminium alloy specimen image after deepening in uninhibited 0.5 M HCl solution for 3 h at 303 K as badly damaged. Aluminium surfaces in corrosive media might reach a maximum height scale of 95.0 nm. Fig. 12(a-b) displayed three-dimensional AFM images of aluminium at 303 K with and without ELE. Without an inhibitor mechanism, the aluminium surface suffered moderate damage from the acidic solution dissolving (Fig. 12a), with a scale maximum of 95.0 nm. The protection coating formation on the surface, which minimizes aluminium roughness and successfully shields aluminium from corrosion, caused the greatest scale of the inhibited aluminium surface to be 34.3 nm at its maximum (Fig. 12b) [52]. With the use of AFM techniques, 3D roughness data were also acquired. Table 7 shows the values of Rmax (maximum roughness), Rq (root mean square roughness), as well as Ra (average roughness) for aluminium surfaces with and without ELE. The larger surface roughness of aluminium causes the Ra, Rq, and Rmax values in an uninhibited system to be higher. Although the values were dramatically reduced by adding 0.5 percent of ELE, it can be ascribed to the dissolving of the oxide coating. The surface seems more level, homogenous, and consistent with the addition of the ELE (Fig. 12b). The symmetry of a surface's changes around the mean line is measured by roughness skewness, which is very sensitive to irregular high peaks or deep valleys. An excellent bearing surface is created by having a negative skewness value [53]. Roughness kurtosis is a statistical indicator of the spike distribution below and above the mean line. The kurtosis values seen in both systems—with and without inhibitor—are more than 3 in favour of spiky surfaces, less than 3 in favour of bumpy surfaces, and 3 in favour of totally random surfaces. These findings showed that the ELE inhibitor has a suitable corrosion resistance level.

The spectrum of ELE was examined to understand the inhibition system better. Fig. 13 reports the FTIR spectrum result of Eucalyptus leaves extract. Eucalyptus leaves extract was examined to assess the primary functional group. It was discovered that the hydroxyl group stretching vibration peak was found in the region of 3200–3500cm−1, the alkyl/aryl ether group C–O stretching vibration peak was seen at 1031.89 and 1220.43cm−1. The group C–C stretching vibration peak was found at 1614cm−1 and This value fall within the range of 1566–1650cm−1. The peak at 2917.54 and 2849.33 cm−1 are denoted to CH alkane. 1513.85 cm−1 for NO stretching for the nitro group.

Commonly, the dissolution of aluminium in an acid solution is due to hydrogen evolution-type occurrence. There is a reaction occurring at the microelectrodes of the corrosion cell, and it goes as follows:
(14)
A l
→
A
l
3 +
+
3
e −
(
a n o d e
)
(15)
H +
+
e −
→
H
(
c a t h o d e
)

Followed by the reaction:
(16)
H
+ H
→
H 2
↑

In varying ELE concentrations, aluminium corrosion in acid solution is retarded. The findings demonstrated that the inhibitory system includes blockage of the aluminium surface by ELE through adsorption. The chemical structure of inhibitors and the metal's nature both impact the phenomena of adsorption [54]. The thermodynamic parameters provide valuable data about the corrosion inhibition mechanism for the adsorption of ELE, which favors physisorption-type adsorption. It reveals that the ELE may electrostatically adsorb on the aluminium surface. Though, the major constrains on the use of these type of inhibitor according to umoren et al. [55] is that plant extracts contain myriads of heteroatoms which makes it cumbersome to pinpoint a definite molecule responsible for inhibition process [56]. The primary part of Eucalyptus is 1,8-cineole, according to the data and consistency with earlier investigations [57]. The reports demonstrated that Eucalyptus's most significant slandered essential oil constituents are α-pinene, β-pinene, Paracymin, Aromadendrene, Bicyclomeracorn, Spatolenol and 1,8-cineole [58]. ELE naturally contain numerous of organic compounds along with the aforementioned molecules that can increase inhibition efficiency. Because the plant extract contains numerous of heteroatoms in their constitutions [59]. These heteroatoms are capable of donating electrons, and the activity of these molecules is attributable to the adsorption of molecules on metal surfaces through a non-shared pair of oxygen atom electrons [60]. The significant delocalization of the electrons on the planarity, aromatic rings, and the existence of lone pairs of electrons on the N- and O- atoms may be responsible for the high inhibitory efficiency, favouring higher extract adsorption on the surface.

In this study, the cost of ethanol extraction of ELE was very low. Thus, the total cost of eucalyptus leaves, labour, water and energy towards the production of ELE extract is about 2 to 2.5 dollars/kg, which is very less compared to that of synthesized organic inhibitor. This study concludes that alcoholic extraction of ELE has a good adsorptive tendency for the aluminium surface.
•
The inhibitory effectiveness rises, and the corrosion rate falls as the ELE concentration rises.
•
In an acid solution, corrosion loss rises as temperature rises. Corrosion loss occurs as temperature rises, but inhibition effectiveness decreases, according to the addition of ELE in corrosive media.
•
Negative values are found for the adsorption of heat and free energy. The entropy of adsorption and activation enthalpy have positive values in all cases.
•
The mean value of 'Ea' with ELE acid solution is greater than the plain acid solution.
•
The inhibition effect seems to be physisorption, and ELE encompasses the cathodic and anodic area by general adsorption, according to the straight-line Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm graphs.
•
The polarization curve shows ELE is a mixed-type inhibitor.
•
AFM as well as SEM methods were applied to validate the protective layer created on the aluminium surface by the adsorption inhibitor ELE in the HCl solution. The ELE emerged as a potential and environmentally friendly natural inhibitor for the corrosion control of Al in 0.5 M Rmax solution.
•
The inhibition efficiency value is estimated with weight loss data, EIS, and polarization processes show high agreement with just a little fluctuation because of the technique's inherent limitations.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the “Department of Chemistry, Arts Science and Commerce College, Kholwad and Shri M R Desai Art and EELK Commerce College , Chikhli”, for providing a laboratory facility and library services.
